title: Map, Set and structured copying

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Map, Set and structured copying; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Map, Set and structured copying
sources.claim: Map stores key-value associations using arbitrary identity keys, while Set stores unique values using SameValueZero equality.

sources.claim: Map stores key-value associations using arbitrary identity keys, while Set stores unique values using SameValueZero equality.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Map stores key-value associations using arbitrary identity keys, while Set stores unique values using SameValueZero equality.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Map uses a DOM node object as a key without string coercion. Your goal is to select and use Array, Object, Map or Set and identify aliasing in a nested copy. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Control flow and functions; pf data selection; Arrays and indexed data; Object records and property access; pf state mutation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Map stores key-value associations using arbitrary identity keys, while Set stores unique values using SameValueZero equality. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Object and Map have different key semantics, iteration order is insertion order, and object keys compare by identity. This draft therefore rejects: Two separate objects with identical properties are one Set value because their contents match.

includes: Choose keyed or unique-value collections and understand spread as a shallow operation.
Select and use Array, Object, Map or Set and identify aliasing in a nested copy.
excludes: Object and Map have different key semantics, iteration order is insertion order, and object keys compare by identity.

outcomes: Select and use Array, Object, Map or Set and identify aliasing in a nested copy.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Object keys use identity in Map and Set; structurally equal but distinct objects remain distinct entries.
